query: Can you point to studies or tasks focused on detecting patronizing and condescending language, particularly in contexts involving vulnerable communities?

title.value: SemEval-2022 Task 4: Patronizing and Condescending Language Detection

authors_year.value: Carla Perez-Almendros, Luis Espinosa-Anke, Steven Schockaert (2022)

source_or_doi.value: DOI: 10.18653/v1/2022.semeval-1.38; Source: Proceedings of the 16th International Workshop on Semantic Evaluation (SemEval-2022); OA: hybrid; Citations: 44

recommendation_reason.value: This paper presents an overview of Task 4 at SemEval-2022, which was focused on detecting Patronizing and Condescending Language (PCL) towards vulnerable communities.

supported_research_question.value: focused on detecting Patronizing and Condescending Language (PCL) towards vulnerable communities

method.value: Two sub-tasks were considered: a binary classification task, where participants needed to classify a given paragraph as containing PCL or not, and a multi-label classification task, where participants needed to identify which types of PCL are present (if any)

main_conclusion.value: summarize the main resulting insights about PCL detection and categorization

limitations.value: Two sub-tasks were considered: a binary classification task, where participants needed to classify a given paragraph as containing PCL or not, and a multi-label classification task, where participants needed to identify which types of PCL are present (if any).

relevance_strength.value: detecting Patronizing and Condescending Language (PCL) towards vulnerable communities

title.value: Don't Patronize Me! An Annotated Dataset with Patronizing and Condescending Language towards Vulnerable Communities

authors_year.value: Carla Pérez-Almendros, Luis Espinosa-Anke, Steven Schockaert (2020)

source_or_doi.value: DOI: 10.48550/arxiv.2011.08320; Source: arXiv (Cornell University); OA: green; Citations: 3

recommendation_reason.value: we introduce a new annotated dataset which is aimed at supporting the development of NLP models to identify and categorize language that is patronizing or condescending towards vulnerable communities (e.g. refugees, homeless people, poor families).

supported_research_question.value: identify and categorize language that is patronizing or condescending towards vulnerable communities

method.value: Our analysis of the proposed dataset shows that identifying PCL is hard for standard NLP models, with language models such as BERT achieving the best results.

data_or_scenario.value: new annotated dataset which is aimed at supporting the development of NLP models to identify and categorize language that is patronizing or condescending towards vulnerable communities (e.g. refugees, homeless people, poor families).

main_conclusion.value: identifying PCL is hard for standard NLP models, with language models such as BERT achieving the best results.

limitations.value: In this paper, we introduce a new annotated dataset which is aimed at supporting the development of NLP models to identify and categorize language that is patronizing or condescending towards vulnerable communities (e.

relevance_strength.value: Don't Patronize Me! An Annotated Dataset with Patronizing and Condescending Language towards Vulnerable Communities

title.value: Pre-Training Language Models for Identifying Patronizing and Condescending Language: An Analysis

authors_year.value: Carla Perez Almendros, Luis Espinosa-Anke, Steven Schockaert (2022)

source_or_doi.value: DOI: 10.63317/28828gdna3y2; Source: https://doi.org/10.63317/28828gdna3y2; OA: gold; Citations: 0

recommendation_reason.value: Patronizing and Condescending Language (PCL) is a subtle but harmful type of discourse, yet the task of recognizing PCL remains under-studied by the NLP community.

supported_research_question.value: we study to what extent PCL detection models can be improved by pre-training them on other, more established NLP tasks.

method.value: pre-training them on other, more established NLP tasks.

data_or_scenario.value: available datasets are limited in size

main_conclusion.value: performance gains are indeed possible in this way, in particular when pre-training on tasks focusing on sentiment, harmful language and commonsense morality.

limitations.value: available datasets are limited in size

relevance_strength.value: Pre-Training Language Models for Identifying Patronizing and Condescending Language
